questions.stem: Variation between organisms is described as:

options.text: A sign that organisms are non-living

questions.explanation: No two individuals are identical in all traits; variation is a defining feature of life and the basis for classification.

questions.stem: The original morphological concept of a species, used by Linnaeus, defined a species as a group of organisms with:
questions.explanation: Linnaeus used the morphological concept, grouping organisms by shared physical traits.

questions.scenario: The scientific name of the human is Homo sapiens.
questions.stem: In this binomial name, what does 'Homo' represent?
questions.explanation: In the binomial system the first name is the genus and the second distinguishes the species.

questions.stem: Which is the correct way to write a binomial name?
questions.explanation: The genus takes an initial capital letter and the species name is lowercase.

questions.stem: According to the biological species concept, members of the same species can:
questions.explanation: The biological species concept defines a species as a group that can breed and produce fertile offspring.

questions.scenario: A horse and a donkey breed to produce a mule, which is sterile.
questions.stem: According to the biological species concept, this shows that horses and donkeys are:
questions.explanation: Because their offspring (the mule) is sterile, they do not produce fertile offspring and are considered separate species.

questions.stem: Speciation is best described as:

options.text: The splitting of one species into two or more
The merging of two species into one

questions.explanation: Speciation is the splitting of one species into two or more, usually gradually.

questions.scenario: Two populations are diverging but can still occasionally interbreed.
questions.stem: Why can it be difficult to decide whether they are one species or two?

options.text: Speciation is gradual, so the boundary can be arbitrary
All populations are always separate species

questions.explanation: Because divergence is gradual, deciding whether diverging populations are the same or different species can be arbitrary.

questions.stem: How many chromosomes do human body cells contain?
questions.explanation: Humans have 46 chromosomes, while chimpanzees have 48.

questions.stem: Why do diploid cells always have an even number of chromosomes?

options.text: There is only ever one chromosome

questions.explanation: Diploid cells contain chromosomes in homologous pairs, so the total is always even.

questions.scenario: A karyogram arranges chromosomes for study.
questions.stem: Chromosomes in a karyogram are classified by all of the following EXCEPT:

options.text: Their base sequence letter by letter

questions.explanation: Karyograms classify chromosomes by banding pattern, length and centromere position, not by full base sequence.

questions.scenario: Humans have 46 chromosomes while other great apes have 48.
questions.stem: The evidence suggests human chromosome 2 arose from:

options.text: The fusion of two ancestral chromosomes

questions.explanation: Evidence suggests human chromosome 2 arose from the fusion of two chromosomes seen separately in a shared primate ancestor.

questions.stem: The genome of an organism is defined as:
questions.explanation: The genome is all the genetic information of an organism.

questions.stem: Small differences in single bases that create variation within a species are called:
questions.explanation: Members of a species share most of their genome, but single-nucleotide polymorphisms give some diversity.

questions.stem: Eukaryotic genomes vary in size, which is determined by:

options.text: The size of the organism only

questions.explanation: Genome size is determined by the total amount of DNA an organism has.

questions.stem: How does genetic variation within a species compare with variation between species?

options.text: Variation between species is much larger
Variation within a species is much larger
There is no variation between species

questions.explanation: Variation between species is much larger than variation within a species.

questions.stem: A potential future use of whole genome sequencing is:
questions.explanation: A potential future use of whole genome sequencing is personalized medicine, tailored to an individual's genome.

questions.stem: A current use of whole genome sequencing is:
questions.explanation: Current uses of whole genome sequencing include research into evolutionary relationships.

questions.scenario: Over recent decades genome sequencing has changed dramatically.
questions.stem: Which trend describes these changes?
questions.explanation: Whole genome sequencing has become faster and cheaper over time.

questions.stem: A species defined only by shared visible characteristics is being defined by the:
questions.explanation: Defining a species by shared physical traits is the morphological concept used by Linnaeus.

questions.stem: Which is a challenge for the biological species concept?

options.text: It cannot easily be applied to organisms that do not reproduce sexually
It works perfectly for every organism
It ignores whether offspring are fertile

questions.explanation: The biological species concept struggles with organisms that do not breed sexually, among other challenges.

questions.stem: The patterns of variation among organisms provide the basis for:
questions.explanation: The complex patterns of variation are the basis for naming and classifying organisms.

questions.stem: Chimpanzees have how many chromosomes in their body cells?
questions.explanation: Chimpanzees have 48 chromosomes, two more than humans.

questions.stem: Two humans differ slightly in their DNA. This is because:

options.text: They share most of their genome but have some variations such as SNPs

questions.explanation: Members of a species share most of the genome, with variation such as SNPs giving individual differences.

questions.stem: The position of the centromere is one feature used to:

options.text: Measure the genome size in bases
Count the number of genes exactly

questions.explanation: Chromosomes are classified in karyograms by banding patterns, length and centromere position.

questions.stem: Besides overall size, eukaryote genomes also vary in their:
questions.explanation: Genomes vary both in overall size and in base sequence.

questions.scenario: A student compares genome sizes from a database across taxonomic groups.
questions.stem: A key finding is that genome size:

options.text: Does not always match organism complexity

questions.explanation: Comparing genome sizes shows that larger genomes do not always correspond to more complex organisms.

questions.scenario: Two organisms are named Quercus robur and Quercus alba.
questions.stem: What can you conclude about them?

options.text: They belong to the same genus and have similar traits

questions.explanation: Sharing the genus name Quercus means they are in the same genus and have similar traits.

questions.stem: Speciation usually happens:

options.text: Instantly in a single generation always

questions.explanation: Speciation usually happens gradually, with populations becoming increasingly different in their traits.

questions.stem: That more than one definition of 'species' exists shows that:

options.text: Competing species concepts are used by biologists
Only one definition has ever existed

questions.explanation: Competing species definitions exist, each with strengths and limitations.

questions.stem: Which statement about individuals within a species is correct?

options.text: No two individuals are identical in all their traits

questions.explanation: Variation is universal: no two individuals are identical in all traits.

questions.stem: The fact that organisms in the same species share most of their genome demonstrates:

options.text: That the species has no diversity
That genomes are all identical between species

questions.explanation: Sharing most of the genome reflects unity within a species, while variations such as SNPs provide diversity.

questions.stem: Different species tend to have different chromosome numbers. This illustrates:

options.text: Diversity in chromosome numbers between species
That chromosomes do not exist in some species

questions.explanation: Chromosome numbers vary between species, part of the diversity of organisms.

questions.scenario: A statement claims chromosome 2 arose by fusion; another claims humans are 'special creations'.
questions.stem: Which statement is testable using scientific evidence?
questions.explanation: The chromosome 2 fusion hypothesis makes predictions that can be tested against genomic evidence, unlike untestable statements.

questions.stem: Why is the binomial naming system useful?

options.text: It gives each species a unique, universally recognised name
It uses a different name in every country
It avoids naming organisms at all

questions.explanation: The binomial system provides each species with a unique, internationally understood two-part name.

questions.stem: Which comparison of genomes is correct?

options.text: Genomes of all eukaryotes are the same size
Base sequences are identical across species

questions.explanation: Genomes vary in size and sequence, with far greater variation between species than within one.

questions.stem: As sequencing costs have fallen, whole genome sequencing has become:

options.text: More widely available for research and medicine

questions.explanation: Falling costs and increasing speed have made whole genome sequencing more accessible for many uses.

questions.scenario: Two populations look identical but never interbreed in the wild.
questions.stem: Under the biological species concept, deciding if they are one species is difficult because:

options.text: Whether they could produce fertile offspring may be unknown

questions.explanation: The biological concept relies on interbreeding and fertile offspring, which may be hard to assess, creating challenges.

questions.stem: Members of the same species in the morphological concept are grouped because they:

options.text: Share a set of common physical traits

questions.explanation: The morphological concept groups organisms with shared traits into a species.

questions.stem: Variation among organisms is best described as:
questions.explanation: Patterns of variation are complex and found in all life, forming the basis of classification.

questions.scenario: Bacteria can transfer genes between different species.
questions.stem: Why does this cause problems for the biological species concept?

options.text: The concept assumes genes stay within a sexually breeding group

questions.explanation: The biological species concept does not work well where genes can be transferred between species, as with horizontal gene transfer in bacteria.

questions.stem: The biological species concept is hard to apply to asexually reproducing organisms because:

options.text: They do not breed sexually to produce fertile offspring
They reproduce too rarely to observe

questions.explanation: Because the concept depends on interbreeding, it does not work well for organisms that reproduce asexually.

questions.scenario: Two closely related species have different chromosome numbers.
questions.stem: Cross-breeding between them is unlikely to produce fertile offspring because:

options.text: Chromosomes cannot pair properly during meiosis
They belong to the same species

questions.explanation: Different parental chromosome numbers prevent proper pairing in meiosis, so fertile offspring are unlikely.

questions.stem: Chromosome number is described as a shared trait within a species because:

options.text: Members of a species typically have the same chromosome number
Every individual has a different number

questions.explanation: Within a species chromosome number is usually the same, acting as a shared trait.

questions.scenario: A student sorts local plants using a series of paired either/or questions.
questions.stem: What tool is being used?
questions.explanation: A dichotomous key identifies organisms through a series of paired (either/or) choices.

questions.scenario: Scientists collect water and analyse the DNA it contains to list the species present.
questions.stem: This technique uses:
questions.explanation: Environmental DNA combined with barcodes allows rapid identification of species and biodiversity in a habitat.

questions.stem: A major advantage of using environmental DNA and barcodes is that biodiversity can be assessed:
questions.explanation: Environmental DNA and barcodes allow the biodiversity of habitats to be investigated rapidly.

questions.stem: Horizontal gene transfer refers to genes being passed:

options.text: Between organisms other than from parent to offspring

questions.explanation: Horizontal gene transfer moves genes between organisms (even between species), not through normal parent-to-offspring inheritance.

questions.stem: A hybrid formed from parents with different chromosome numbers is often:
questions.explanation: Mismatched chromosome numbers usually make hybrids sterile because chromosomes cannot pair correctly in meiosis.

questions.scenario: In a dichotomous key, each step offers two contrasting options.
questions.stem: Following the key correctly leads to:
questions.explanation: Working through the paired choices of a dichotomous key leads to the identification of the organism.
